coredata.dc:title: Exploring quantum confinement signature in nitrogen-functionalized graphene quantum dots: Effective mass approximation (EMA) model insights from computational and experimental analyses
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Permatasari, Fitri Aulia
Umami, Reza
Mayangsari, Tirta Rona
Sustini, Euis
Aimon, Akfiny Hasdi
Noor, Fatimah Arofiati
Hapidin, Dian Ahmad
Surtiyeni, Neni
Iskandar, Ferry
Khairurrijal, Khairurrijal
coredata.dc:description:
A well-established relationship between bandgap energy and Quantum Dots (QDs) dimension, predicted by Effective Mass Approximation (EMA) models, is a cost-effective and reliable method to evaluate the bandgap energy of QDs. The EMA model sheds light on linearity between bandgap energy (
E
g a p
)
to their inverse square diameter (
1 /
d n
), as a signature of the quantum confinement, with the n value reflecting the dimension of the quantum confinement. This study systematically investigated the power parameter "n" dependence in the bandgap energy of Nitrogen-functionalized Graphene Quantum Dots (N-GQDs) with distinct C–N configurations, i.e., Pyridinic-N, Pyrrolic-N, Graphitic-N based on computational and experimental data. The results showed that the calculated bandgap energy values reveal a notable weakening of the size dependence in N-GQDs compared to pristine GQDs, particularly in Pyrrolic-N GQDs, indicative of a unique geometric dimensionality of confinement. This systematic exploration advances our understanding of "n" dependence and bandgap energy in N-GQDs, laying a solid groundwork for modulating the bandgap energy of N-GQDs for optoelectronics and quantum device applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bandgap energy
EMA model
N-GQDs
Quantum confinement

R.U. would like to thank the Ministry of Fiscal Indonesia Endowment Fund for Education (LPDP) for her doctoral scholarship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The optimized geometries of GQDs in varying diameter size (6.15 Å - 17.30 Å) and C–N Configuration with 7.6 % Nitrogen content (Pyridinic-N, Pyrrolic-N, and Graphitic-N). (b) The calculated UV–Vis spectra of pristine GQDs in varying sizes. (c) The calculated UV–Vis spectra of N-GQDs in varying C–N configurations with GQDs size of 17.30 Å.
Calculated energy level diagram of the Pristine GQDs and N-GQDs.
The density of states of the pristine GQDs (a) and the carbonyl-GQDs-17A (b). The vertical dashed line indicates the position of the HOMO level. A Gaussian width of 0.05 a.u. is used.

(a) Illustration of the N-GQDs sample that was prepared by bottom-up method (hydrothermal). (b–d) N1s and their deconvoluted XPS spectra of the Pyridinic-N GQDs (b), Pyrrolic-N GQDs (c), and Graphitic-N GQDs (d).
TEM image and their corresponding size distribution of the (a) Pyridinic-N, (b) Pyrrolic-N, (c) Graphitic-N.

The Tauc plot of the N-GQDs was extracted from their reflectance spectra. (a) Pyridinic-N GQDs. (b) Pyrrolic-N GQDs. (c) Graphitic-N GQDs. (d) The relation between bandgap energy and diameter size of GQDs (experimental and theoretical results).

The band gap energy vs size of the N-GQDs. The dashed line is the linear fit of the band gap energy E
gap of GQDs to the 1/d
n .

head.ce:title: Exploring quantum confinement signature in nitrogen-functionalized graphene quantum dots: Effective mass approximation (EMA) model insights from computational and experimental analyses

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A well-established relationship between bandgap energy and Quantum Dots (QDs) dimension, predicted by Effective Mass Approximation (EMA) models, is a cost-effective and reliable method to evaluate the bandgap energy of QDs. The EMA model sheds light on linearity between bandgap energy (
E
g a p
)
to their inverse square diameter (
1 /
d n
), as a signature of the quantum confinement, with the n value reflecting the dimension of the quantum confinement. This study systematically investigated the power parameter "n" dependence in the bandgap energy of Nitrogen-functionalized Graphene Quantum Dots (N-GQDs) with distinct C–N configurations, i.e., Pyridinic-N, Pyrrolic-N, Graphitic-N based on computational and experimental data. The results showed that the calculated bandgap energy values reveal a notable weakening of the size dependence in N-GQDs compared to pristine GQDs, particularly in Pyrrolic-N GQDs, indicative of a unique geometric dimensionality of confinement. This systematic exploration advances our understanding of "n" dependence and bandgap energy in N-GQDs, laying a solid groundwork for modulating the bandgap energy of N-GQDs for optoelectronics and quantum device applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is an emergent fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterial in size of below 10 nm [1–3]. Since GQDs was discovered in 2004, it has been the subject of extensive computational and experimental investigations due to versatile applications across conversion energy, electronic devices, optoelectronic devices, biomedicine, catalysts, and sensors. As one of the Quantum Dots (QDs) materials, the GQDs could also possess the quantum confinement phenomenon, providing a well-designed bandgap energy by controlling their diameter size.

The relationship between band gap energy and the size of QDs has long been established, primarily predicted through the Effective Mass Approximation model proposed by Brus [4]. In this model, the signature of the quantum confinement is the linearity between bandgap energy (
E
g a p
)
and their inverse square diameter (
1 /
d 2
). Other approaches, including density functional theory (DFT) [5]and DFT-derived EMA [6] could also offer a robust means for predicting bandgap energy. Nonetheless, these methods required substantial computational resources and time, prompting the consideration that the EMA model represents an economical and reliable approach to address the QDs’ bandgap energy induced by a quantum confinement effect [7–10].

The signature of the quantum confinement, linearity between
E
g a p
to their inverse square carbon cluster number
(
1 /
N 2
)
, have been experimentally reported in GQDs that are below 2 nm by Ji et al. [11]. Nevertheless, many experimental and computational studies revealed that energy can also be finely tuned in the N-GQDs’ band gap by manipulating their C–N configuration, i.e., Pyridinic-N, Pyrrolic-N, Graphitic-N [12–16]. These C–N configuration groups have been simultaneously formed during the bottom-up synthesis [17–20], a facile and promising preparation method. Therefore, it is a captivating element of debate and uncertainty surrounding the manifestation of quantum confinement signatures, as predicted by the EMA model on N-GQDs.

Integrated experimental and computational studies have been utilized to unravel the relation of bandgap energy to the nanomaterials dimension in various materials [21–24]. Based on their studies, the signature of the quantum confinement is the linearity between bandgap energy (
E
g a p
)
to the geometric dimensionality of the confinement, as
1 /
d n
, with the power parameter “n” is less than 2 [21–24]. For instance, the bandgap energy of the CdSe quantum wires and InP QDs were proportional to
1 /
d 1.36
and
1 /
d 1.35
, respectively [21,24]. Further study was also conducted for the Black Phosphorus QDs, the as-adjusted EMA model has been proposed for both computational and experimental studies. It has been revealed that the quantum confinement was experimentally weaker than theoretically, indicated by the power parameter “n” which is around 0.95 and 0.41 for the experiment and DFT results, respectively. This geometric dimensionality of the confinement could be manifested by their structure, such as layer thickness and numbers.

To the best of our knowledge, no study has comprehensively assessed the relationship between the power parameter "n" and the bandgap energy in the context of N-GQDs. Given the versatile electronic and optical properties of N-GQDs attributed to C–N configurations, a systematic exploration of the dependency of the power mentioned above parameter "n" in the case of N-GQDs is imperative. Therefore, an integrated systematic experimental and computational approach was conducted in this work to elucidate the quantum confinement effect on N-GQDs structure. This investigation holds significant implications for advancing our understanding of N-GQDs' bandgap energy modulation in a cost-effective and reliable approach, paving the way for developing novel applications in optoelectronics and quantum devices.

In this work, we developed the model of GQDs using polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) molecules of different sizes, as shown in Fig. 1 . Pristine GQDs consist of carbon atoms and terminated hydrogen atoms at the edges (Fig. 1a), while the N-GQDs consist of C–N configurations in three possible types, i.e., N-pyridinic, N-pyrrolic, N-graphitic (Fig. 1b). All GQDs models were maintained at sp2 hybridization to avoid the influence of sp2/sp3 hybridization to their properties. The optimized model of GQDs was calculated using density functional theory (DFT) under the water environment by polarizable continuum model (PCM) solvation. The optical and electronic properties were determined using a 6-311G** basis set with B3LYP (Becke three parameters hybrid functional with Lee-Yang-Perdew correlation functionals) with (B3LYP/6-311G**). All calculations were accomplished using the Gaussian 09 software package. All optimized structures have converged and gained positive oscillation frequency. The absorbance spectra and HOMO-LUMO energies in excited states were analyzed via Chemcraft 1.8 [25]. The visualizations of the density of the state were rendered using Multiwfn 3.7 [26].

The Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) were prepared using a hydrothermal route using small molecular, citric acid, and urea [12,19,20]. In brief, citric acid (C6H8O7, 99.5 %, Sigma-Aldrich) and urea (CO(NH2)2, 98.5 %, Sigma-Aldrich) were dissolved in distilled water at room temperature for 15 min. The citric acid to urea molar ratio was varied to selectively control the C–N configuration types on the GQDs. Then, the precursor was transferred into the Teflon inner hydrothermal reactor and heated for 4–6 h. The reaction temperature also varied from 140 to 200 °C. The obtained samples were purified by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and filtered using a 0.22 μm regenerated cellulose filter (Sartorius Stedim Biotech Co.). For further characterization, the samples were dried by freeze drying for three days.

The sample morphology was characterized using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). The powder samples were diluted in distilled water (0.01 mg/mL) and dropped onto the Cu TEM Grid. TEM images were obtained using a Hitachi TEM (HT-7700, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 80 kV. The chemical composition was characterized by X-ray Photoemission Spectroscopy (XPS) using Thermo Scientific K-Alpha XPS (Thermo Scientific, USA). The high-resolution spectra were deconvoluted using CasaXPS with a Shirley background. The bandgap of samples was estimated from reflectance spectra measured by Diffuse Reflectance Spectroscopy (Specord Plus Bio-serv, Analytik Jena, Germany) at room temperature.

The GQDs in varying sizes and C–N configurations were systematically studied computationally and experimentally, as shown in Fig. 1. As the GQDs' model is one of the pivotal factors affecting the optical and electronic properties of GQDs [27–31], the pristine GQDs were modelled using PAH molecules in which the size was varied from 6.15 Å for C16H10 to 17.30 Å for C96H24 (Fig. 1a). It is worth notifying that the GQDs that were prepared through bottom-up methods from small organic molecules, e.g., citric acid, urea, ethylenediamine, etc., subsequently functionalized by various types of Nitrogen. To investigate the effect of various C–N configurations and GQDs' size on the optical and electronic properties of GQDs, the nitrogen position was consistently adjusted on the edge (Fig. 1a) since the Nitrogen mid position has been reported to affect the GQDs' properties [12]. The Nitrogen concentration was fixed at 7.6 % to represent the nitrogen concentration of our as-prepared samples. In varying GQDs’ sizes, DFT calculation revealed that all of these structures were in the planar and stable phase, excluding the geometric distortion influence on their optical and electronic properties [32]. However, in varying C–N configurations, the Pyrrolic-N GQDs showed a bending structure that might be attributed to the high electron density from Pyrrolic-N [12]. It is worth noting that the distorted structure of GQDs was also observed in the presence of other atoms in a high concentration [33].

To evaluate the side effects of GQDs on their optical properties, their calculated absorption spectra are shown in Fig. 1b and c. In the case of pristine GQDs, even in varying sizes, the absorption spectra consistently exhibit two main peaks with a redshifted as increasing the GQDs’ diameter sizes (Fig. 1b). These peaks in the region 200–300 nm correspond to the π→π* transition of CC bonds, indicating the presence of the aromatic carbon core [34,35]. The shifting might be related to the alteration of the energy gap in the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbitals (HOMO) and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) induced by the different sizes of polyaromatic carbon core. Interestingly, in varying C–N configurations, more than two prominent peaks were observed (Fig. 1c). For instance, the pyridinic-N GQDs showed an additional peak at ∼590 nm, which indicates the presence of n→π* of C–N or CN bonds [36]. Moreover, the additional absorption peak might also correspond to the alteration of its band gap energy induced by the frontier orbital hybridization phenomenon of the N-GQDs [13,14,29,37]. In the case of N-GQDs, the broadening absorption peaks were observed that might be primarily attributed to the high probability of optical transitions between closely spaced energy levels. In smaller-sized GQDs, the higher ratio of nitrogen atoms, particularly in structures with high surface-to-volume ratios, significantly affects energy levels due to their electron-donating or -withdrawing capabilities. This increased presence of nitrogen amplifies optical transitions, leading to broader absorption spectra. The pronounced broadening of the absorption peaks is thus a combined effect of nitrogen doping and quantum confinement. These consequences of C–N configurations to the absorption spectra of GQDs have been well-documented experimentally and theoretically [12,38–41].

The alteration of absorption properties induced by the size of GQDs and nitrogen presence in GQDs can be explained through the energy level diagrams of pristine- and N-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 2 . This finding agrees with the previous studies in which the band gap energy could modulate the absorption spectrum of GQDs via size, edge, and doped-heteroatom [42–44]. For instance, the alteration of the absorption spectrum of the N-GQDs concerning that of the pristine GQDs is consistent with the alterations of their band gap energies. The band gap energy of the N-GQDs is smaller than that of the pristine GQDs of the same size. A molecular orbital analysis containing the percentage of atomic contribution to their molecular orbital was tabulated in Table S1. For all N-GQD types, N atoms contribute to both occupied and unoccupied orbital. This condition indicates the frontier orbital hybridization that induces a shift in HOMO-LUMO energy levels on N-GQDs [44,45]. For instance, the Pyridinic N atoms contribute to occupied and unoccupied orbital in relatively similar values (∼0.2), inducing an even shift of the HOMO-LUMO through a frontier orbital hybridization. As a result, the bandgap of pyridinic-N is not reduced significantly compared to their pristine GQDs. In contrast, the N atoms on pyrrolic-N contribute unevenly to the occupied (∼0.08) and unoccupied orbitals (∼0.01), inducing an uneven shift on the HOMO-LUMO level. Thus, the bandgap energy of N-GQDs was reduced than the pristine GQDs.

A clear illustration of size and C–N configuration effects on GQDs can be obtained by analyzing their density of states (DOS). Fig. 3 depicts a total and partial DOS for each atom on the pristine- and N-GQDs. Note that as described by the energy diagrams in Fig. 2, the N-GQDs inherit uneven shifts of HOMO and LUMO energy levels resulting in a reduced band gap energy that is smaller than that of the pristine GQDs. The contribution of the C–N configuration is also consistent with the partial DOS of N atoms in Fig. 3b–d. Nitrogen atoms introduce additional electronic states within the band structure, especially near a fermi level. This alteration is attributed to the changes in the electronic configuration and hybridization of nitrogen orbitals with carbon orbitals.

It is important to note that three main consensuses explain nanostructures' optical and electronic properties, especially GQDs [46,47]. First, the pioneer consensus is the quantum confinement effect that can tune the band gap energy of GQDs by its size [48]. Second, the band gap energy of GQDs can also be dictated by the functional group on its surface, especially C–N configurations [49–51]. As reported in our previous studies, the presence of nitrogen altered the bandgap energy of carbon-based nanostructures through the LUMO-HOMO energy level shifting induced by frontier orbital hybridization [41,52]. Third, the sp2/sp3 ratio hybridization of GQDs could also adjust its band gap energy [53]. However, in this study, the influence of sp2/sp3 ratio hybridization on its band gap energy can be excluded because all the GQDs structures were maintained in sp2 hybridization. Therefore, the influences of the quantum confinement effect and C–N configurations can predominantly control the optical properties and band gap energy of GQDs presented in Figs. 1 and 2.

Experimentally, both size and C–N configuration effects were coupled and undistinguishable since the C–N configuration presences could alter their bond length and enlarge their size. Furthermore, complex preparation and purification techniques are required to selectively control the C–N configurations on GQDs. Then, three selected GQD samples were prepared to represent the N-GQDs in this work. A bottom-up preparation method generates a sample with three possible C–N configurations, as illustrated by Fig. 4 a. The X-ray Photoemission Spectroscopy (XPS) characterization was conducted to evaluate their C–N configurations. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectra were deconvoluted into three peaks: sp2 carbons C═C (285 eV), sp3 carbons C–O/C–N (286.7 eV), and C═O/C═N (287.5 eV) (see Fig. S1). This shows that the samples are mainly composed of sp2 hybridization. It is worth noting that the hybridization factor on the Carbon-based QDs could influence the optical and electronic properties of QDs due to their delocalization electron [54]. However, the significance of the sp3 hybridization in this context might be underscored by its manifestation in the C–N configuration types. To further analyze the C–N configuration in the samples, high-resolution N1s XPS spectra were evaluated. The spectra were deconvoluted into three potential C–N configurations centred at ∼399 eV, ∼400 eV, and ∼401.5 eV, which correspond to Pyridinic-N, Pyrrolic-N, and Graphitic-N, respectively [55]. A slight shift in the peaks indicated that each sample mainly consisted of a specific C–N configuration (see Fig. 4b–d). Therefore, representative samples were selected based on their dominant characteristics.

N-GQDs morphologies were characterized by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), as their results are shown in Fig. 5 . Three samples are spherical, with average diameters as much as (3.78 ± 0.78) nm, (3.01 ± 0.34) nm, and (3.26 ± 0.57) nm for Pyridinic-N GQDs, Pyrrolic-N GQDs, and Graphitic-N GQDs, respectively (Fig. 5 a-f). A smaller deviation standard of the as-prepared samples' distribution size indicates a homogeneous sample size. The Pyrrolic-N has a relatively smaller average diameter size than the Pyridinic-N and Graphitic-N, in line with the DFT results that showed the bending geometry of the Pyrrolic-N (Fig. 1a), decreasing their planar diameter size. All samples are smaller than 10 nm, indicating that the quantum confinement effect might have occurred in these samples, altering their bandgap energy. However, these samples' dominant C–N configuration might also alter their bandgap energy. Therefore, a comprehensive study is needed to address these issues.

With the Tauc plot method's help, the samples' bandgap energy was experimentally estimated from the reflectance spectra obtained from the Diffuse Reflectance Spectroscopy (DRS) measurement, as shown in Fig. 6 a-c. The Tauc plot method assumes that the energy-dependent absorption coefficient (α) can be expressed by the following equation [56],
(1)
(
F
(
R ∞
)
.
h ν
)
1 / γ
= B
(
h ν −
E
g a p
)
where
F
(
R ∞
)
is the Kubelka-Munk function,
h
is the Planck constant,
ν
is the photon frequency,
B
is the constant,
E
g a p
is the band gap energy, and
γ
is the factor depends on the nature of the electron transition (direct or indirect bandgap). The
(
F
(
R ∞
)
.
h ν
)
2
is plot as the incident photon energy (
h ν
) to extract the allowed direct transition energy which is indicated by the intercept linear fits. The band structure calculations that confirm the direct bandgap nature of GQDs have been extensively reported and documented in the literature [57–60]. Accordingly, in this study, it has been assumed that the synthesized GQDs possess a direct bandgap. Based on this method, the estimated band gap energy of the Pyridinic-N GQDs, Pyrrolic-N GQDs, and Graphitic-N GQDs are 2.07 eV, 1.49 eV, and 1.25 eV, respectively (Fig. 6a–c). The relation curve showed that the bandgap energy dependence on the diameter size of GQDs is not well-aligned with the theoretical EMA model (Fig. 6d), indicating the non-ideal quantum confinement in N-GQDs.

The quantum confinement effect takes place when the nanostructure has a diameter size smaller than its excitonic Bohr radius. In the case of GQDs, the quantum confinement is supposed to occur in sizes of 1–20 nm [61]. The size dependence of the band gap energy is formulated by the effective mass approximation (EMA) model proposed by Brus et al. [4]:
(2)
Δ E
g
(
G Q D s
)
=
(
3
ℏ 2
π
2
m *
d 2
)
−
1.78
e 2
2 π
ε 0
ε R
d
where
Δ E
g
is the band gap energy causing by quantum confinement,
ℏ
is the reduced Planck constant,
d
is the size of GQDs,
m *
is the effective mass of system,
e
is the electron charge,
ε 0
and
ε R
are the free space permittivity and relative permittivity, respectively. Equation (2), also considering the shielded Coulomb interaction, is derived using the Hamiltonian model, assuming the potential box as the barrier potential [4]. This equation clearly indicates that the band gap energy of GQDs increases as its size decreases. Theoretically, the band gap energy is asymptotic toward the band gap energy of its bulk [62]. However, experimentally, the observed value of band gap energy does not perfectly scale with
1 /
d 2
. This discrepancy might arise from the parabolic energy band and infinite potential barrier [63]. Then, the band gap energy
E
g a p
(
G Q D s
)
is regarded as varying with
1 /
d n
as a function of the composition and geometric dimensionality of the confinement barrier [22]. Throwing light on the “
n
” dependence, various
n
values for
1 /
d n
were calculated as shown in Fig. 7 . In the case of N-GQDs, the best fit is given by
n = 1.64 , 1.08 , and
1.47
, for the Pyridinic-N GQDs, Pyrrolic-N GQDs, and Graphitic-N GQDs, respectively. It indicates that the size dependence of the band gap energy in N-GQDs is weaker than that in the pristine GQDs.

Among three possible C–N configurations, the size dependence of bandgap in Pyrrolic-N is, therefore, weaker than others, indicating a high discrepancy in their geometric dimensionality of confinement barrier [22]. It might correspond to their high-density charge through the carbon structure, inducing a ‘bowl-like’ structure while lowering the confinement dimensionality [12]. Thus, this discrepancy in the size dependence of the band gap energy should be considered in the band gap energy estimation by the EMA model, especially for carbon nanostructures with the presence of a C–N configuration group. Our experimental results could also be compared to those of the as-adjusted EMA models, as long as the known gap energy of bulk Nitrogen-doped Graphene structure. As reported elsewhere, the bandgap energy of N-doped graphene varied based on their C–N configuration and concentration, by 0.3–2.2 eV [64–66]. These findings indicate that the N configuration could also contribute to the tailoring energy gap of the GQDs, but still represent linearity between bandgap energy and the quantum confinement dimensionality, a signature of quantum confinement, as manifested in the as-adjusted EMA model. An as-adjusted EMA model could facilitate well-designed tunable bandgap energy and optical properties of N-GQDs, crucial for bio-imaging and optoelectronic devices, enabling precise control over emission wavelengths of both luminescence and phosphorescence [67,68]. Furthermore, the decreased bandgap energies and enhanced charge carrier mobility, especially in Pyrrolic-N GQDs, facilitate efficient manipulation of quantum states, making them highly suitable for quantum computing and communication applications. Due to their high environmental sensitivity, these properties also render N-GQDs ideal for developing selective and sensitive sensors.

In brief, this study unveils the nuanced relationship between the power parameter "n" and bandgap energy on the EMA model for Nitrogen-functionalized Graphene Quantum Dots (N-GQDs), specifically with three possible C–N configurations i.e., Pyridinic-N, Pyrrolic-N, and Graphitic-N. The results showed that the power parameter "n" is given by
n = 1.64 , 1.08 , and
1.47
, for the Pyridinic-N GQDs, Pyrrolic-N GQDs, and Graphitic-N GQDs, respectively. The lower value indicating weakening of size dependence, which was observed particularly notably in Pyrrolic-N GQDs, highlights a unique geometric dimensionality of confinement. This study's success lies in bridging computational predictions with experimental observations, instilling confidence in the reliability of the as-adjusted EMA model for nuanced exploration of quantum confinement effects in carbon nanostructures, guiding future research and applications.
Not Applicable.
Not Applicable.

Fitri Aulia Permatasari: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Reza Umami: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation. Tirta Rona Mayangsari: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Euis Sustini: Writing – review & editing. Akfiny Hasdi Aimon: Writing – review & editing. Fatimah Arofiati Noor: Writing – review & editing. Dian Ahmad Hapidin: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Neni Surtiyeni: Writing – review & editing. Ferry Iskandar: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Conceptualization. Khairurrijal Khairurrijal: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is fully supported by Research Grant “Riset PPMI KK FMIPA 2023 Institut Teknologi Bandung”, Grant Number FMIPA.PPMI-1-80-2023. R.U. would like to thank the Ministry of Fiscal Indonesia Endowment Fund for Education (LPDP) for her doctoral scholarship.
